jokes.text: That sign says, 'applesauce.' No. No. No, I'm kidding. It says, 'applause.'
jokes.setup: Hank references an applause sign
jokes.punchline: Deliberately mishears it as 'applesauce' before correcting himself
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank - earnest but slightly dim warm-up host persona. The misdirection works because it's so unnecessary and perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: That sign says, 'applesauce.' No. No. No, I'm kidding. It says, 'applause.'

jokes.text: and now, because he desperately needs your approval, Larry Sanders!
jokes.setup: Traditional late-night host introduction format
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest psychological assessment instead of flattering intro
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment that captures the show's premise - the gap between public face and private insecurity. Perfectly structured subversion of intro conventions.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: and now, because he desperately needs your approval, Larry Sanders!

jokes.text: The buyer came in During the evening And, uh, found only $20 in the register And passed.
jokes.setup: Network sale rumors being addressed seriously
jokes.punchline: Implies the network is so worthless it's like a failed corner store
jokes.explanation: Good structural joke with solid economy. The register metaphor works well for industry insiders.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: The buyer came in During the evening And, uh, found only $20 in the register And passed.

jokes.text: Her husband maury Has more credibility As far as I'm concerned.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Connie Chung reporting the story
jokes.punchline: Suggests daytime TV host Maury Povich is more credible than network news anchor
jokes.explanation: Decent industry joke that plays on the contrast between serious news and tabloid TV.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Her husband maury Has more credibility As far as I'm concerned.

jokes.text: Welcome to the monkey house. Pass the penis, please. Am I right?
jokes.setup: Tense meeting about network sale
jokes.punchline: Completely inappropriate and nonsensical non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: Pure Artie - vulgar, inappropriate, makes no sense but perfectly captures his character's crude attempts at wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Welcome to the monkey house. Pass the penis, please. Am I right?

jokes.text: Later i'll pass out Some transcripts So we can all Try to figure out What Hank meant At our leisure.
jokes.setup: Artie's incomprehensible monkey house comment
jokes.punchline: Treating it like it's profound enough to require study
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that extends the previous joke while showcasing Larry's dry wit and the absurdity of the situation.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Later i'll pass out Some transcripts So we can all Try to figure out What Hank meant At our leisure.

jokes.text: When people lie, They tend to cover their mouths. I learned that From jack webb when I was on dragnet, 1970.
jokes.setup: Artie giving Larry advice about lying to staff
jokes.punchline: Cites very specific, dated TV detective knowledge as expertise
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Artie's tendency to treat pop culture as real expertise. The specific year adds authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: When people lie, They tend to cover their mouths. I learned that From jack webb when I was on dragnet, 1970.

jokes.text: What are you listed under, Hank or Kingsley? Or hey, now?
jokes.setup: Hank admits he's in Hollywood madam's black book
jokes.punchline: Suggesting he'd use his catchphrase as a prostitute alias
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that connects Hank's professional persona with his personal shame. The catchphrase as hooker name is inspired character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: What are you listed under, Hank or Kingsley? Or hey, now?

jokes.text: It was during The mid-eighties, And I was carrying Some excess weight. And I had a persistent Skin rash, And it was hard for me To get dates.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to explain his prostitute use
jokes.punchline: Oversharing increasingly pathetic personal details
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - the escalating TMI creates mounting discomfort. Skin rash detail is particularly cringe-worthy.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It was during The mid-eighties, And I was carrying Some excess weight. And I had a persistent Skin rash, And it was hard for me To get dates.

jokes.text: A late-night news magazine Wrap-up of the day's events, But with a hip, Mtv sensibility.
jokes.setup: Dick proposing show format changes
jokes.punchline: Generic, buzzword-heavy TV executive speak that means nothing
jokes.explanation: Solid industry satire of executive babble, though somewhat predictable. Works because it's so recognizably corporate.

dialogue.speaker: Dick
dialogue.line: A late-night news magazine Wrap-up of the day's events, But with a hip, Mtv sensibility.

jokes.text: We can't make enough Of that shit.
jokes.setup: Dick explaining his beer marketing philosophy
jokes.punchline: Crude dismissal of his own product while praising the marketing
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Dick's cynical approach to business. The profanity adds authenticity to his crude nature.

dialogue.speaker: Dick
dialogue.line: We can't make enough Of that shit.

jokes.text: I see what's wrong. You're drunk.
jokes.setup: Dick's increasingly bizarre beer analogies
jokes.punchline: Larry's matter-of-fact diagnosis of the situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry deadpan delivery. The economy is excellent - just five words that cut through all the nonsense.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I see what's wrong. You're drunk.

jokes.text: Yes! Fuck that beer farm! We are out of here!
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about working for Dick
jokes.punchline: Agent's over-the-top enthusiasm and calling the network a 'beer farm'
jokes.explanation: Good agent character work - the mix of profanity and agricultural metaphor captures Hollywood agent excess.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yes! Fuck that beer farm! We are out of here!

jokes.text: I-- I live For this shit. It gets me all Hot and bothered.
jokes.setup: Agent excited about getting Larry out of his contract
jokes.punchline: Inappropriately sexual language about business conflict
jokes.explanation: Typical sleazy agent behavior - sexualizing business. Character-appropriate but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I-- I live For this shit. It gets me all Hot and bothered.

jokes.text: You didn't see us. You saw nothing. You heard nothing. It's not what It looks like.
jokes.setup: Staff member discovers Larry with his agent
jokes.punchline: Larry acts like they were caught in an affair instead of a business meeting
jokes.explanation: Solid paranoia comedy that shows Larry's tendency to make situations more suspicious by overreacting.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You didn't see us. You saw nothing. You heard nothing. It's not what It looks like.

jokes.text: the new Larry Sanders show, afternightline. Unfortunately, That's the actual title They wanna use.
jokes.setup: Agent pitching ABC opportunity
jokes.punchline: Horrible punning title that agent admits is terrible
jokes.explanation: Good TV industry joke about terrible network naming conventions. The 'unfortunately' sells the self-awareness.

dialogue.line: the new Larry Sanders show, afternightline. Unfortunately, That's the actual title They wanna use.

jokes.text: Great idea for a sitcom.
jokes.setup: Larry threatens to move to Montana and live in a shack
jokes.punchline: Agent treats existential breakdown as pitch material
jokes.explanation: Excellent agent character comedy - the complete inability to separate life from business opportunities. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Great idea for a sitcom.

jokes.text: You ever see A scorsese film? Yeah. You know, Like goodfellas? Yeah.
jokes.setup: Artie explaining why he can't go to New York
jokes.punchline: Using mob movies as reference point for his actual criminal past
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that plays on Artie's gruff persona while building tension about his mysterious past.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You ever see A scorsese film?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You know, Like goodfellas?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: What if we shoot In new jersey? Even worse. They live there.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to find compromise on New York issue
jokes.punchline: New Jersey is worse because that's where the mob actually lives
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke with solid geography humor. The matter-of-fact delivery adds to the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What if we shoot In new jersey?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Even worse. They live there.

jokes.text: Hey, butt-bongo. Bravo!
jokes.setup: Howard Stern arriving
jokes.punchline: Artie's crude congratulations referencing Howard's infamous bit
jokes.explanation: Captures Artie's crude humor and the show's willingness to reference real Howard Stern content.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Hey, butt-bongo. Bravo!

jokes.text: You're a big fan? Hey, take your top off.
jokes.setup: Meeting an eager fan
jokes.punchline: Howard immediately turns inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Accurate Howard Stern characterization showing his boundary-pushing persona, though fairly predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Howard
dialogue.line: You're a big fan? Hey, take your top off.

jokes.text: The door that's marked 'can.'
jokes.setup: Howard asks to use the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Larry's overly literal direction
jokes.explanation: Mild character moment showing Larry's tendency toward uncomfortable precision in awkward situations.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: The door that's marked 'can.'

jokes.text: It's like she's Got something Shoved up her ass All the time. It's like she's On the perpetual rag.
jokes.setup: Howard commenting on Larry's girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Increasingly crude and inappropriate observations
jokes.explanation: Authentic Howard character work but relies heavily on shock value. The escalation works for character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Howard
dialogue.line: It's like she's Got something Shoved up her ass All the time. It's like she's On the perpetual rag.

jokes.text: And I really think You should move The show to new york.
jokes.setup: Larry's been stressed about potential NY move
jokes.punchline: Girlfriend enthusiastically supports the thing he's been dreading
jokes.explanation: Good ironic timing that adds relationship tension at the worst possible moment for Larry.

dialogue.speaker: Jancis
dialogue.line: And I really think You should move The show to new york.

jokes.text: I think It'd be really good for us To have some time apart.
jokes.setup: Jancis pushing for NY move
jokes.punchline: Reveals this is actually about breaking up
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that reveals the real motivation behind her NY enthusiasm, adding personal stakes to professional crisis.

dialogue.speaker: Jancis
dialogue.line: I think It'd be really good for us To have some time apart.

jokes.text: well, I can reveal, for the first time, ladies and gentlemen... Larry Sanders will be taking his show to new york
jokes.setup: Larry has been keeping NY move secret
jokes.punchline: Howard broadcasts it to his entire radio audience
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that destroys Larry's careful secrecy in the most public way possible. Perfect character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Howard
dialogue.line: well, I can reveal, for the first time, ladies and gentlemen... Larry Sanders will be taking his show to new york

jokes.text: He's taking us To new york. What if he's not Taking all of us?
jokes.setup: Staff hearing about NY move on radio
jokes.punchline: Immediate panic about job security
jokes.explanation: Good workplace panic comedy showing how rumors immediately turn to self-preservation.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: He's taking us To new york.

dialogue.speaker: Darlene
dialogue.line: What if he's not Taking all of us?

jokes.text: For making me Look like a liar In front of The entire staff. It was A real pleasure.
jokes.setup: Beverly sarcastically thanking Larry
jokes.punchline: For the privilege of being humiliated
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic delivery that shows Beverly's frustration with being left out of the loop.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: For making me Look like a liar In front of The entire staff. It was A real pleasure.

jokes.text: You got a huge ass. It's a huge, huge ass. That's why we sent for That new lens from sony, So we could Cover that huge ass.
jokes.setup: Artie telling Larry what's really on his mind
jokes.punchline: Brutal honesty about Larry's appearance with technical solution
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Artie moment - the escalation and technical detail make the insult funnier. Perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You got a huge ass. It's a huge, huge ass. That's why we sent for That new lens from sony, So we could Cover that huge ass.

jokes.text: I have nightmares Of someone like you Coming through My window.
jokes.setup: Larry talking to Chris Farley
jokes.punchline: Honest admission about finding Chris frightening
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry honesty - inappropriate but genuine reaction. Very economical and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I have nightmares Of someone like you Coming through My window.

jokes.text: You're not going to amount To jack squat! You're gonna end up Eatin' a steady diet Of government cheese And livin' in a van Down by the river!
jokes.setup: Chris doing his motivational speaker character
jokes.punchline: The classic 'van down by the river' escalation
jokes.explanation: Classic Chris Farley bit that became culturally iconic. Perfect structure with escalating despair culminating in the memorable river line.

dialogue.speaker: Chris Farley
dialogue.line: You're not going to amount To jack squat! You're gonna end up Eatin' a steady diet Of government cheese And livin' in a van Down by the river!

jokes.text: I flew all the way in From new york for this. You know?
jokes.setup: Larry cutting Chris's segment short
jokes.punchline: Chris's disappointed reaction about the travel
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing the awkwardness of cutting a guest short, with Chris's genuine disappointment.

dialogue.speaker: Chris Farley
dialogue.line: I flew all the way in From new york for this. You know?

jokes.text: I knew it! It's new york!
jokes.setup: Chris mentions flying from New York
jokes.punchline: Phil thinks this confirms the NY move rumors
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows how paranoid the staff has become about reading signs into everything.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I knew it! It's new york!

jokes.text: this is the final Larry Sanders show That you've watched tonight
jokes.setup: Larry making what sounds like a farewell speech
jokes.punchline: Turns out to be a fake-out series finale
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta moment that plays with audience expectations. The misdirection is perfectly executed with authentic farewell speech structure.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: this is the final Larry Sanders show That you've watched tonight

jokes.text: The name's darlene, You fucking idiot.
jokes.setup: Larry giving generic thanks to unnamed staff
jokes.punchline: Staff member angrily corrects him
jokes.explanation: Perfect puncturing of Larry's fake sentiment. The profanity and specificity make it land harder.

dialogue.speaker: Darlene
dialogue.line: The name's darlene, You fucking idiot.

jokes.text: The name's darlene, You fucking idiot.
jokes.setup: Larry giving generic thanks to staff
jokes.punchline: Darlene's angry correction and insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect puncture of Larry's fake sentimentality. Maximum economy and impact - complete character in one line.

dialogue.speaker: Darlene
dialogue.line: The name's darlene, You fucking idiot.

jokes.text: You're a miserable fucker. A real miserable fucker.
jokes.setup: Larry's fake finale speech
jokes.punchline: Artie's assessment of Larry's character
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie response - brutal honesty delivered with characteristic bluntness. The repetition adds emphasis.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You're a miserable fucker. A real miserable fucker.

jokes.text: * Da da, da-da, da da * And now, because He made a big mistake... Larry Sanders.
jokes.setup: Larry humming his theme song alone in Montana
jokes.punchline: Gives himself the same brutal intro Hank gave him
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback to the opening that shows Larry's self-awareness and isolation. The character integration is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: * Da da, da-da, da da * And now, because He made a big mistake... Larry Sanders.
